3/8   Derivatives are Informative

Proposition 2

Let I ‚äÜ R be an open interval. Suppose f has a derivative at x ‚ąą I. (a) If Df (x) > 0, then there exists őī > 0 such that f is strictly increasing on (x ‚ąí őī, x + őī). (b) If Df (x) < 0, then there exists őī > 0 such that f is strictly decreasing on (x ‚ąí őī, x + őī).

Df (x)

f

Proof See suggested exercises.

4/8

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚ąą R. ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).

5/8

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚ąą R. ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).

‚ó¶ What if h = f √ó g?

5/8

Suppose both f and g are differentiable at x ‚ąą R. ‚ó¶ One can show that h = f + g is also differentiable at x, and further Dh(x) = Df (x) + Dg(x).

‚ó¶ What if h = f √ó g?

Proposition 3

Let f and g be two real-valued functions defined on an open interval I ‚äÜ R. If f and g are differentiable at x, then so is the product function h = f √ó g. Moreover,

Dh(x) = Df (x) g(x) + f (x) Dg(x).

= Df (x)g(x) + f (x)Dg(x).

6/8   The Dreaded Chain Rule

Proposition 4

Let f : I ‚Üí R and g : J ‚Üí R, where I and J are open subsets of R and f (I) ‚äÜ J. If f is differentiable at x ‚ąą I and g is differentiable at y = f (x) ‚ąą J, then the composite function h = g ‚ó¶ f is differentiable at x and satisfies

Dh(x) = Dg(f (x)) Df (x).

7/8   The Dreaded Chain Rule

Proposition 4

Let f : I ‚Üí R and g : J ‚Üí R, where I and J are open subsets of R and f (I) ‚äÜ J. If f is differentiable at x ‚ąą I and g is differentiable at y = f (x) ‚ąą J, then the composite function h = g ‚ó¶ f is differentiable at x and satisfies

Dh(x) = Dg(f (x)) Df (x).

Proof Notice

g(f (u)) ‚ąí g(f (x)) g(f (u)) ‚ąí g(f (x)) f (u) ‚ąí f (x) = . u‚ąíx f (u) ‚ąí f (x) u‚ąíx

Now let u ‚Üí x and use the continuity of f at x.

‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚ąą I, refer to it as the second derivative of f at x and write D2 f (x).

8/8

‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚ąą I, refer to it as the second derivative of f at x and write D2 f (x).

‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative everywhere in the interval I, say f is twice differentiable on I.

‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative at x ‚ąą I, refer to it as the second derivative of f at x and write D2 f (x).

‚ó¶ If Df admits a derivative everywhere in the interval I, say f is twice differentiable on I.

Treat D2 f itself as a function defined on I
